MATH 341 — Differential Equations

Methods & Techniques Quick Reference

Author

University of Scranton, Mathematics Department

Published

May 29, 2026

Note

Textbook: Logan, J.D. A First Course in Differential Equations, 3rd ed. (Springer, 2015).
Sections covered: All sections except §2.6.
Course website: https://math341diffeq.netlify.app/

1 First-Order Equations (Chapter 1)

1.1 Quick Decision Guide

Form of Equation Technique See Section
\(y' = f(x)\,g(y)\) Separation of Variables §1.2
\(y' + p(x)\,y = q(x)\) Integrating Factor §1.3
\(M\,dx + N\,dy = 0\), \(M_y = N_x\) Exact Equation §1.4
\(y' = f(y/x)\) Substitution \(v = y/x\) §1.5
\(y' = f(ax + by + c)\) Substitution \(v = ax+by+c\) §1.5
\(y' + p(x)\,y = q(x)\,y^n\) Bernoulli Substitution §1.5
None of the above Numerical Methods (§1.6) or Qualitative Analysis §1.1

1.2 Separation of Variables (§1.2)

When to use: The right-hand side factors as \(f(x) \cdot g(y)\).

Form: \(\dfrac{dy}{dx} = f(x)\,g(y)\)

Steps:

  1. Rewrite as \(\dfrac{dy}{g(y)} = f(x)\,dx\) (check \(g(y) \neq 0\)).
  2. Integrate both sides: \(\displaystyle\int \frac{dy}{g(y)} = \int f(x)\,dx + C\).
  3. Solve for \(y\) (if possible); apply initial condition to find \(C\).
Warning

Watch out: Always check for equilibrium solutions where \(g(y) = 0\) — these may be lost when dividing.

Example: \(y' = xy\)
\(\Rightarrow \dfrac{dy}{y} = x\,dx \Rightarrow \ln|y| = \dfrac{x^2}{2} + C \Rightarrow y = Ae^{x^2/2}\)

1.3 Integrating Factor / Linear First-Order (§1.3)

When to use: Equation is linear in \(y\) (or can be put in standard linear form).

Standard form: \(y' + p(x)\,y = q(x)\)

Steps:

  1. Identify \(p(x)\) and \(q(x)\).
  2. Compute integrating factor: \(\mu(x) = e^{\int p(x)\,dx}\).
  3. Multiply both sides by \(\mu\): \(\big(\mu\,y\big)' = \mu\,q(x)\).
  4. Integrate: \(\mu\,y = \displaystyle\int \mu\,q(x)\,dx + C\).
  5. Solve: \(y = \dfrac{1}{\mu}\left(\displaystyle\int \mu\,q\,dx + C\right)\).
Tip

Tip: The general solution is \(y = y_h + y_p\) where \(y_h = C/\mu\) is the homogeneous solution and \(y_p = \frac{1}{\mu}\int \mu q\,dx\) is a particular solution.

Example: \(y' - 2xy = x\)
\(p(x) = -2x\), \(\mu = e^{-x^2}\), then \((e^{-x^2}y)' = xe^{-x^2}\), giving \(y = -\tfrac{1}{2} + Ce^{x^2}\).

1.4 Exact Equations (§1.4)

When to use: Equation written as \(M(x,y)\,dx + N(x,y)\,dy = 0\) with \(\dfrac{\partial M}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial N}{\partial x}\).

Steps:

  1. Check exactness: \(M_y = N_x\)?
  2. Find potential function \(F(x,y)\) such that \(F_x = M\), \(F_y = N\).
  3. Integrate \(M\) w.r.t. \(x\): \(F = \displaystyle\int M\,dx + g(y)\).
  4. Differentiate \(F\) w.r.t. \(y\) and set equal to \(N\) to find \(g'(y)\); integrate.
  5. Solution: \(F(x,y) = C\).
Warning

Not exact? Try integrating factors \(\mu(x)\) or \(\mu(y)\) to make it exact.
If \(\dfrac{M_y - N_x}{N}\) depends only on \(x\), then \(\mu = e^{\int\frac{M_y-N_x}{N}dx}\).

1.5 Substitution Methods (§1.5)

1.5.1 Homogeneous Equations

Form: \(y' = f\!\left(\dfrac{y}{x}\right)\)
Substitution: \(v = y/x\), so \(y = vx\) and \(y' = v + xv'\).
Reduces to a separable equation in \(v\) and \(x\).

1.5.2 Bernoulli Equations

Form: \(y' + p(x)\,y = q(x)\,y^n\), \(n \neq 0, 1\)
Substitution: \(w = y^{1-n}\), so \(w' = (1-n)y^{-n}y'\).
Reduces to a linear equation in \(w\).

1.5.3 Linear Substitution

Form: \(y' = f(ax + by + c)\)
Substitution: \(v = ax + by + c\), so \(v' = a + by'\).
Reduces to a separable equation.

1.6 Autonomous Equations & Qualitative Analysis (§1.1, §1.6)

Form: \(y' = f(y)\) (right side depends only on \(y\))

Key concepts:

  • Equilibria (critical points): Solve \(f(y^*) = 0\).
  • Stability:
    • \(f'(y^*) < 0\): stable (attractor)
    • \(f'(y^*) > 0\): unstable (repeller)
  • Phase line: Plot \(f(y)\) vs. \(y\); arrows point right where \(f > 0\), left where \(f < 0\).
Tip

Tip: The phase line immediately reveals long-term behavior without solving the ODE explicitly.

1.7 Numerical Methods (§1.7)

1.7.1 Euler’s Method

Formula: \(y_{n+1} = y_n + h\,f(x_n, y_n)\), where \(h\) is the step size.

Error: \(O(h)\) per step — first-order method. Use small \(h\) for accuracy.

1.7.2 Improved Euler (Heun’s Method)

Predictor: \(\tilde{y}_{n+1} = y_n + h\,f(x_n, y_n)\)
Corrector: \(y_{n+1} = y_n + \dfrac{h}{2}\big[f(x_n, y_n) + f(x_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})\big]\)

Error: \(O(h^2)\) — second-order method.

1.7.3 Runge-Kutta (RK4)

\[y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{6}(k_1 + 2k_2 + 2k_3 + k_4)\] \[k_1 = f(x_n, y_n), \quad k_2 = f\!\left(x_n+\tfrac{h}{2}, y_n+\tfrac{h}{2}k_1\right)\] \[k_3 = f\!\left(x_n+\tfrac{h}{2}, y_n+\tfrac{h}{2}k_2\right), \quad k_4 = f(x_n+h, y_n+hk_3)\]

Error: \(O(h^4)\) — standard workhorse for numerical ODEs.

2 Second-Order Linear Equations (Chapter 2)

2.1 Quick Decision Guide

Equation Type Technique See Section
\(ay'' + by' + cy = 0\) Characteristic Equation §2.1
\(ay'' + by' + cy = g(t)\), \(g\) = poly/exp/sin/cos Undetermined Coefficients §2.3
\(ay'' + by' + cy = g(t)\), \(g\) = other Variation of Parameters §2.4
\(x^2y'' + xy' + \alpha y = 0\) Cauchy–Euler Equation §2.5
Two solutions \(y_1, y_2\) needed Reduction of Order §2.2

2.2 Homogeneous Linear Equations: Characteristic Equation (§2.1)

Standard form: \(ay'' + by' + cy = 0\)

Characteristic equation: \(ar^2 + br + c = 0\), with roots \(r = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}\).

Discriminant Roots General Solution
\(b^2 - 4ac > 0\) Two real: \(r_1 \neq r_2\) \(y = C_1 e^{r_1 t} + C_2 e^{r_2 t}\)
\(b^2 - 4ac = 0\) Repeated: \(r_1 = r_2 = r\) \(y = (C_1 + C_2 t)\,e^{rt}\)
\(b^2 - 4ac < 0\) Complex: \(r = \alpha \pm \beta i\) \(y = e^{\alpha t}(C_1 \cos\beta t + C_2 \sin\beta t)\)
Tip

For complex roots \(r = \alpha \pm \beta i\): \(\alpha = -b/(2a)\), \(\beta = \sqrt{4ac - b^2}/(2a)\).

2.3 Reduction of Order (§2.2)

When to use: One solution \(y_1\) is known; find a second linearly independent solution \(y_2\).

Substitution: \(y_2 = v(t)\,y_1(t)\)

Steps:

  1. Substitute \(y = v\,y_1\) into the ODE.
  2. Use \(y_1'' + p(t)y_1' + q(t)y_1 = 0\) to eliminate terms.
  3. Let \(w = v'\) to get a first-order linear ODE in \(w\).
  4. Solve for \(w\), then integrate to find \(v\), then \(y_2 = v\,y_1\).

Wronskian: \(W(y_1, y_2) = y_1 y_2' - y_1' y_2 \neq 0\) confirms linear independence.

Abel’s Formula: \(W(t) = W(t_0)\,e^{-\int p(t)\,dt}\)

2.4 Undetermined Coefficients (§2.3)

When to use: \(ay'' + by' + cy = g(t)\) where \(g(t)\) is a combination of polynomials, exponentials, sines, or cosines.

General solution: \(y = y_h + y_p\)

2.4.1 Particular Solution Guesses

Forcing \(g(t)\) Guess for \(y_p\)
\(P_n(t)\) (degree-\(n\) poly) \(A_n t^n + \cdots + A_1 t + A_0\)
\(e^{\alpha t}\) \(Ae^{\alpha t}\)
\(\sin(\beta t)\) or \(\cos(\beta t)\) \(A\cos(\beta t) + B\sin(\beta t)\)
\(e^{\alpha t}\sin(\beta t)\) or \(e^{\alpha t}\cos(\beta t)\) \(e^{\alpha t}(A\cos\beta t + B\sin\beta t)\)
\(P_n(t)\,e^{\alpha t}\) \((A_n t^n + \cdots + A_0)\,e^{\alpha t}\)
Warning

Modification Rule: If any term of the guess solves the homogeneous equation, multiply the entire guess by \(t\) (or \(t^2\) if needed for repeated roots).

2.5 Variation of Parameters (§2.4)

When to use: \(y'' + p(t)y' + q(t)y = g(t)\) when \(g(t)\) is not of the “undetermined coefficients” type, or the coefficients are non-constant.

Requires: Two known linearly independent solutions \(y_1\), \(y_2\) of the homogeneous equation.

Formulas: \[y_p = -y_1 \int \frac{y_2\,g}{W}\,dt + y_2 \int \frac{y_1\,g}{W}\,dt\]

where \(W = W(y_1, y_2) = y_1 y_2' - y_1' y_2\).

Note

Always write the ODE in standard form (leading coefficient 1) before applying this formula.

2.6 Cauchy–Euler Equations (§2.5)

Form: \(x^2 y'' + \alpha x y' + \beta y = 0\), \(x > 0\)

Substitution: \(x = e^t\) (or try \(y = x^r\))

Characteristic equation: \(r(r-1) + \alpha r + \beta = 0 \Rightarrow r^2 + (\alpha - 1)r + \beta = 0\)

Roots General Solution
Two real: \(r_1 \neq r_2\) \(y = C_1 x^{r_1} + C_2 x^{r_2}\)
Repeated: \(r_1 = r_2 = r\) \(y = x^r(C_1 + C_2 \ln x)\)
Complex: \(r = \lambda \pm \mu i\) \(y = x^\lambda\big(C_1 \cos(\mu \ln x) + C_2 \sin(\mu \ln x)\big)\)

2.7 Applications: Spring-Mass System (§2.1–§2.3)

Equation of motion: \(my'' + \gamma y' + ky = F(t)\)

Parameter Meaning
\(m > 0\) Mass
\(\gamma \geq 0\) Damping coefficient
\(k > 0\) Spring constant
\(F(t)\) External forcing

Damping regimes (unforced, \(F = 0\)):

Condition Regime Behavior
\(\gamma^2 - 4mk > 0\) Overdamped Exponential decay, no oscillation
\(\gamma^2 - 4mk = 0\) Critically damped Fastest decay without oscillation
\(\gamma^2 - 4mk < 0\) Underdamped Oscillates with decaying amplitude
\(\gamma = 0\) Undamped Pure oscillation at \(\omega_0 = \sqrt{k/m}\)
Warning

Resonance: Occurs when \(F(t) = F_0\cos(\omega_0 t)\) and \(\gamma = 0\). The amplitude grows without bound — use the Modification Rule (multiply guess by \(t\)).

3 Laplace Transforms (Chapter 3)

3.1 Definition & Key Properties

\[\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = F(s) = \int_0^\infty e^{-st} f(t)\,dt, \quad s > 0\]

Property Formula
Linearity \(\mathcal{L}\{af + bg\} = aF(s) + bG(s)\)
Derivative \(\mathcal{L}\{f'\} = sF(s) - f(0)\)
Derivative \(\mathcal{L}\{f''\} = s^2F(s) - sf(0) - f'(0)\)
\(n\)-th Derivative \(\mathcal{L}\{f^{(n)}\} = s^n F(s) - s^{n-1}f(0) - \cdots - f^{(n-1)}(0)\)
\(s\)-shifting \(\mathcal{L}\{e^{at}f(t)\} = F(s-a)\)
\(t\)-shifting \(\mathcal{L}\{u_c(t)f(t-c)\} = e^{-cs}F(s)\)
Convolution \(\mathcal{L}\{f * g\} = F(s)\,G(s)\)
Multiplication by \(t\) \(\mathcal{L}\{t\,f(t)\} = -F'(s)\)

3.2 Common Laplace Transform Table

\(f(t)\) \(F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f\}\) Condition
\(1\) \(\dfrac{1}{s}\) \(s > 0\)
\(t^n\) \(\dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}\) \(s > 0\)
\(e^{at}\) \(\dfrac{1}{s-a}\) \(s > a\)
\(\sin(bt)\) \(\dfrac{b}{s^2+b^2}\) \(s > 0\)
\(\cos(bt)\) \(\dfrac{s}{s^2+b^2}\) \(s > 0\)
\(e^{at}\sin(bt)\) \(\dfrac{b}{(s-a)^2+b^2}\) \(s > a\)
\(e^{at}\cos(bt)\) \(\dfrac{s-a}{(s-a)^2+b^2}\) \(s > a\)
\(t^n e^{at}\) \(\dfrac{n!}{(s-a)^{n+1}}\) \(s > a\)
\(u_c(t)\) (unit step at \(c\)) \(\dfrac{e^{-cs}}{s}\) \(s > 0\)
\(\delta(t - c)\) (Dirac delta) \(e^{-cs}\)

3.3 Solving IVPs with Laplace Transforms (§3.1–§3.3)

When to use: Constant-coefficient ODEs (any order), especially with discontinuous/impulsive forcing.

Steps:

  1. Take \(\mathcal{L}\) of both sides; use initial conditions automatically.
  2. Solve algebraically for \(Y(s) = \mathcal{L}\{y(t)\}\).
  3. Use partial fractions to decompose \(Y(s)\).
  4. Apply \(\mathcal{L}^{-1}\) (use table) to find \(y(t)\).
Note

Partial Fractions Reminder:

  • Distinct linear factors: \(\dfrac{A}{s-a} + \dfrac{B}{s-b}\)
  • Repeated factor: \(\dfrac{A}{s-a} + \dfrac{B}{(s-a)^2}\)
  • Irreducible quadratic: \(\dfrac{As+B}{s^2+b^2}\) — complete the square if needed

3.4 Step Functions & Piecewise Forcing (§3.2)

Heaviside (unit step) function: \[u_c(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < c \\ 1, & t \geq c \end{cases}\]

Writing piecewise functions: Any piecewise function can be written using \(u_c\): \[f(t) = \begin{cases} g(t), & t < c \\ h(t), & t \geq c \end{cases} = g(t) + [h(t) - g(t)]\,u_c(t)\]

Key formula: \(\mathcal{L}\{u_c(t)\,f(t-c)\} = e^{-cs}F(s)\)
(shift the function, then multiply by \(e^{-cs}\))

3.5 Impulse Functions & Dirac Delta (§3.3)

Dirac delta: \(\delta(t - c)\) models an instantaneous impulse at \(t = c\).

\[\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t-c)\} = e^{-cs}, \quad c \geq 0\]

\[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\,\delta(t-c)\,dt = f(c)\]

Physical meaning: An impulsive force (hammer blow, lightning strike) applied at time \(t = c\).

3.6 Convolution (§3.4)

Definition: \((f * g)(t) = \displaystyle\int_0^t f(t-\tau)\,g(\tau)\,d\tau\)

Convolution Theorem: \(\mathcal{L}\{f * g\} = F(s)\,G(s)\)

When to use: Invert a product \(F(s)G(s)\) when partial fractions are unwieldy, or express solutions to IVPs with general forcing as a convolution integral.

4 Linear Systems (Chapter 4)

4.1 System Setup

A first-order linear system: \[\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{b}(t), \quad \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n\]

For a homogeneous system (\(\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{0}\)), seek solutions \(\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{v}\,e^{\lambda t}\), giving the eigenvalue problem \(A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}\).

4.2 Quick Decision Guide: \(2 \times 2\) Systems

Eigenvalues of \(A\) Solution Form Phase Portrait
Real, distinct, same sign \(C_1\mathbf{v}_1 e^{\lambda_1 t} + C_2\mathbf{v}_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}\) Node (stable if \(\lambda < 0\), unstable if \(\lambda > 0\))
Real, distinct, opposite sign \(C_1\mathbf{v}_1 e^{\lambda_1 t} + C_2\mathbf{v}_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}\) Saddle (always unstable)
Repeated real \(\lambda\) See below Improper node
Complex \(\lambda = \alpha \pm \beta i\) \(e^{\alpha t}(\ldots\cos\beta t + \ldots\sin\beta t)\) Spiral or center

4.3 Solving \(2 \times 2\) Homogeneous Systems (§4.1–§4.2)

Steps:

  1. Find eigenvalues: \(\det(A - \lambda I) = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda^2 - \text{tr}(A)\lambda + \det(A) = 0\).
  2. For each \(\lambda\), find eigenvector \(\mathbf{v}\): \((A - \lambda I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}\).
  3. Write general solution.

4.3.1 Case 1: Distinct Real Eigenvalues

\[\mathbf{x}(t) = C_1\mathbf{v}_1 e^{\lambda_1 t} + C_2\mathbf{v}_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}\]

4.3.2 Case 2: Complex Eigenvalues \(\lambda = \alpha \pm \beta i\)

Compute one complex solution \(\mathbf{x}_1 = e^{\alpha t}(\mathbf{p}\cos\beta t - \mathbf{q}\sin\beta t)\) using the real and imaginary parts of the complex eigenvector \(\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{p} + i\mathbf{q}\): \[\mathbf{x}(t) = C_1 e^{\alpha t}(\mathbf{p}\cos\beta t - \mathbf{q}\sin\beta t) + C_2 e^{\alpha t}(\mathbf{p}\sin\beta t + \mathbf{q}\cos\beta t)\]

4.3.3 Case 3: Repeated Eigenvalue \(\lambda\) (defective)

One eigenvector \(\mathbf{v}\); find generalized eigenvector \(\mathbf{w}\): \((A - \lambda I)\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{v}\). \[\mathbf{x}(t) = C_1\mathbf{v}\,e^{\lambda t} + C_2(\mathbf{v}\,t + \mathbf{w})\,e^{\lambda t}\]

4.4 Phase Plane Analysis (§4.3)

Trace-Determinant Plane: For \(A = \begin{pmatrix}a & b \\ c & d\end{pmatrix}\), let \(\tau = \text{tr}(A) = a+d\) and \(\Delta = \det(A) = ad - bc\).

Region Eigenvalue Type Equilibrium
\(\Delta < 0\) Real, opposite signs Saddle (unstable)
\(\Delta > 0\), \(\tau^2 - 4\Delta > 0\), \(\tau < 0\) Real, both negative Stable node
\(\Delta > 0\), \(\tau^2 - 4\Delta > 0\), \(\tau > 0\) Real, both positive Unstable node
\(\Delta > 0\), \(\tau^2 - 4\Delta < 0\), \(\tau < 0\) Complex, \(\alpha < 0\) Stable spiral
\(\Delta > 0\), \(\tau^2 - 4\Delta < 0\), \(\tau > 0\) Complex, \(\alpha > 0\) Unstable spiral
\(\Delta > 0\), \(\tau = 0\) Pure imaginary Center (stable)

4.5 Nonhomogeneous Systems: Variation of Parameters (§4.4)

System: \(\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{f}(t)\)

Fundamental matrix: \(\Phi(t)\) — columns are linearly independent solutions of \(\mathbf{x}' = A\mathbf{x}\).

Particular solution: \[\mathbf{x}_p(t) = \Phi(t)\int \Phi(t)^{-1}\mathbf{f}(t)\,dt\]

General solution: \(\mathbf{x}(t) = \Phi(t)\mathbf{c} + \mathbf{x}_p(t)\)

5 Nonlinear Systems & Stability (Chapter 5)

5.1 Linearization at Equilibria (§5.1)

System: \(\mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})\), equilibrium at \(\mathbf{x}^* = (x^*, y^*)\) where \(\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}\).

Jacobian matrix at \(\mathbf{x}^*\): \[J = \begin{pmatrix} \partial F_1/\partial x & \partial F_1/\partial y \\ \partial F_2/\partial x & \partial F_2/\partial y \end{pmatrix}\Bigg|_{\mathbf{x}^*}\]

Linearized system near \(\mathbf{x}^*\): \(\mathbf{u}' = J\mathbf{u}\) where \(\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*\).

Note

The stability of \(\mathbf{x}^*\) for the nonlinear system matches the Jacobian classification — except when the Jacobian gives a center (\(\tau = 0\), \(\Delta > 0\)): the nonlinear system may be a center or a spiral. Further analysis (e.g., Lyapunov function) is needed.

Steps:

  1. Find all equilibria: \(F_1(x,y) = 0\) and \(F_2(x,y) = 0\) simultaneously.
  2. Compute \(J\) and evaluate at each equilibrium.
  3. Find eigenvalues of \(J\); classify stability.
  4. Sketch phase portrait near each equilibrium.

5.2 Stability Definitions (§5.1–§5.2)

Type Definition
Stable Nearby trajectories stay nearby
Asymptotically stable Nearby trajectories stay nearby and converge to \(\mathbf{x}^*\)
Unstable Trajectories move away from \(\mathbf{x}^*\)

5.3 Lyapunov Functions (§5.2)

When to use: Proving stability/instability without solving the system; especially useful when linearization is inconclusive (center case).

A function \(V(\mathbf{x})\) is a Lyapunov function for \(\mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})\) near \(\mathbf{x}^*\) if:

  • \(V(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0\) and \(V(\mathbf{x}) > 0\) for \(\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{x}^*\) (positive definite).
  • \(\dot{V} = \nabla V \cdot \mathbf{F} \leq 0\) along trajectories.
Condition on \(\dot{V}\) Conclusion
\(\dot{V} \leq 0\) \(\mathbf{x}^*\) is stable
\(\dot{V} < 0\) \(\mathbf{x}^*\) is asymptotically stable
\(\dot{V} > 0\) (with \(V(\mathbf{x}^*)=0\)) \(\mathbf{x}^*\) is unstable
Tip

Common Lyapunov candidates: \(V = x^2 + y^2\), \(V = ax^2 + by^2\), energy functions \(H(x,y)\).

5.4 Limit Cycles & Periodic Orbits (§5.3)

Limit cycle: An isolated closed trajectory in the phase plane.

Poincaré–Bendixson Theorem: If a trajectory of \(\mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x})\) in \(\mathbb{R}^2\) remains in a bounded region containing no equilibrium, then it must approach a closed orbit (limit cycle).

Bendixson’s Criterion (no limit cycles): If \(\dfrac{\partial F_1}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial F_2}{\partial y}\) has constant sign in a simply connected region, then there are no closed orbits in that region.

6 Overall Strategy Flowchart

Given an ODE/System:
│
├─ Single ODE, 1st order?
│   ├─ Separable? → Separation of Variables
│   ├─ Linear? → Integrating Factor
│   ├─ Exact? → Exact Equations
│   ├─ Bernoulli? → Bernoulli Substitution
│   ├─ Homogeneous (y/x)? → Substitution v = y/x
│   ├─ Autonomous? → Phase Line / Qualitative Analysis
│   └─ None of above → Numerical Methods (Euler / RK4)
│
├─ Single ODE, 2nd order, linear, const. coefficients?
│   ├─ Homogeneous → Characteristic Equation
│   └─ Nonhomogeneous?
│       ├─ g(t) = poly/exp/sin/cos → Undetermined Coefficients
│       └─ Other g(t) → Variation of Parameters
│
├─ Cauchy-Euler? → Substitution x = e^t
│
├─ Use Laplace Transform when:
│   ├─ Initial conditions given
│   ├─ Forcing is piecewise (step functions)
│   └─ Impulse forcing (Dirac delta)
│
└─ System of ODEs?
    ├─ Linear, const. coefficients → Eigenvalue Method
    │   ├─ Distinct real → Standard solution
    │   ├─ Complex → Euler's formula
    │   └─ Repeated → Generalized eigenvectors
    ├─ Nonhomogeneous → Variation of Parameters
    └─ Nonlinear → Linearize at equilibria (Jacobian)
        └─ Stability unclear? → Lyapunov Functions
NoteQuick Reminders
  • Always verify your solution satisfies the original ODE.
  • Apply initial/boundary conditions after finding the general solution.
  • For systems, always check whether the phase portrait classification from the trace-determinant plane matches your computed eigenvalues.
  • When using Laplace transforms, write the ODE in standard form before taking the transform.

MATH 341 — University of Scranton — Fall Semester
Based on Logan, J.D., A First Course in Differential Equations, 3rd ed., Springer, 2015.
